sentences.text: My name is [?
Gerti ?] Gertrude Weiss.
My-- my maiden name is Feuer.
That's F-E-U-E-R.
I was born in Satu Mare.
It was Romania at that time.
Satu Mare is also known as Szatmarnemeti, the Hungarian name.
My birthday is February 28, 1925.
We have three wonder sons, my three sons, our of pride and joy.
We are really very fortunate that they turned out three wonderful human beings.
Unfortuately, none of them live here, two live in New York in Brooklyn, and one lives in Pittsburgh.
My mother and father both were deported, taken to Auschwitz, and to the crematorium.
And I had eight sisters and brothers, because my mother was a second wife of my father's.
The first mother was an older sister.
And my grandmother wanted very much that my mother should marry-- should marry the-- the brother-in-law, because the children should not have a step-mother.
So there was quite an age difference between them.
And actually, we are not only step-- step-brothers and sisters, but cousins.
Satu Mare, in Romania, is relatively-- was a big city, and relatively, an important city, one of the major cities in Romania.
When I grew up, as I said, it was Romania.
But before the First World War, it was part of Austro-Hungary It was part of the Austro-Hungaian Empire.
So I grew up there, and it was a prominent, very prosperous Jewish community.
It numbered about 15,000.
And it was a very nice Jewish life.
Tell me about that life.
First of all, tell me about the shul that you would go to, about the synagogue you would go to.
There were several large shuls in-- in Satu Mare.
The one where-- there was a main shul, which stands today-- a few years ago when I was there, it stands.
Of course, it's practically in ruins.
But you could see the-- the former glory of it.
And the shul that we went to was another shul, not this main shul, where-- which was a large shul.
You know, here in Los Angeles, you could see a lot of Orthodox shuls, little rooms and things.
It was nothing of the sort.
It was a very nice shul.
It was very nice.
And the-- and the first floor was for the men and the upper floor for the women.
But a beautiful building, and built as a shul.
My father was a grain merchant.
And he was in Satu Mare during the Austro-Hungarian rule.
Jews were not allowed to live in the city proper.
They lived in provinces.
And they-- then they-- you know, they were occupied with selling stuff, having little stores, and providing for the peasants things.
But only 100 years before we were deported, in the 1800s, when Franz Joseph-- who was a friend of the Jews-- allowed-- permitted the Jews to-- to move into Satu Mare.
And the my father's family with four other families but among the first families who lived in the city proper.
And I was born under-- under the same address where my father was, only a different house-- my father built the house.
But that-- that place, that lot, was under the Feuer name over 100 years.
The house?
Well, relatively, you know, to America standards, it was a small and modest house.
But it was very-well built from brick.
We had four bedrooms.
We had a big veranda, which had a-- a glass wall where we made the [INAUDIBLE] usually.
Part of it, you could lift the ceiling.
And that's where we had the [INAUDIBLE].
And we had a-- like a-- a living room-- at home, we call them differently-- and-- and a-- and a dining room.
We had a very large family.
And many of-- of the relatives lived in-- in a smaller place, in a village.
Because my mother comes from get village-- my mother who is a descendent of-- who was a descendant of a very famous rabbi, as I mentioned you, [INAUDIBLE].
I want to show you the Sefer, the book, and it will explain everything about my ancestry.
So-- and many of them had always something to shop for and some official things to take care of in the city.
So there was almost never that we-- that we didn't have company-- who lived overnight, you know.
Always, we had.
We always had them.
We-- we were 13, 14, 15 by the table, that was, almost.
And during Pesach, we had the Jewish soldiers for the Seder.
Because the Jewish boys had to be soldiers, too.
And Los Angeles had the big garrison, had the big-- there was a lot of soldiers stationed there.
Yeah, Satu Mare.
And they-- and they couldn't go home, so Jews invited the soldiers to-- to spend the Seder and Passover.
My mother, but we always had a maid to help.
You know, there-- every-- every Jewish home, that I remember, had.
Because they usually had a large family.
Cooking and cleaning was a much harder, different job than in America.
So you needed help.
In fact, when we were young, we had another-- two-- another, younger good girl who took out the younger children for a walk and for-- because my mother didn't have time for that.
When we were very young, we all lived in the same house.
But not for long, because my oldest brother married.
There was quite a bit of difference between my older brother and myself, as I-- as I mentioned from two mothers.
My mother lived in [INAUDIBLE].
That was a village in-- about three hours away by bus, because the train-- the train didn't go there, from Satu Mare.
They were big landowners.
They owned a tremendous amount of the land.
And they had a mill which-- which made flour, and an oil press, and oil, and they had orchards.
And it was a big [GERMAN], as they call it the German, a big house, home.
And my father was a city boy.
And I remember the story, that he-- that I'll never forget because he mentioned it many times, that he never was in a village, and he couldn't imagine.
And when he came to see his future wife, they picked him up in another small town.
He came by train.
And they picked him up with a beautiful carriage, with a beautiful horse-drawn carriage.
And when he arrived in that village, and he saw that house, his smile remained open, because he thought the village is a village, you know?
And he-- so that's what it was.
In my childhood, the biggest treat was to spend some time in the summer in that village.
But my grandmother, and with numerous relatives, cousins, and aunts and uncles, it was the biggest treat.
That-- so big orchards.

They-- in the-- in the late summer, when they harvested the-- the fruits, the plums, and they made-- they had a-- like a-- like a room where they dried the prunes-- where they dried the plums, and they became prunes.
Also, they had a big iron kettle, which was-- which was built into the ground.
Stirred.
And the they made-- they made jam out of it, you know.
But a huge amount, you know, because-- it was.
So, you know, in the night, sitting around the deck.
It was already chill-- chilly, the air.
It was something that I shall always remember.
And I was considered a city girl, so it was a big treat.
Oh, yes.
The games, I don't remember.
I remember we went almost every day to some kind of excursion, to some orchard somewhere.
We took a long picnic-like, and we spent the day there.
And also, what I shall never remember, when the cattle-- the-- the cows came home in the evening-- They were out in the pasture during the day.
And they left-- they were picked up early in the morning, at dawn.
And in the early evening, when they came home, there was one man who was watching them and guiding them.
But each one turned in-- into their own yard.
They knew it.
The cows knew where to go.
It was-- it was just, for me-- I-- I'll never forget it.
No.
First I went elementary school, to the Hebrew school.
Which, I told you, it was a very nice building.
So when I came to this country and my children went, of course, to Hebrew school here, the building, it was a big disappointment.
In America, my children to-- to-- to go to school in such a small-- you know.
It was a beautiful, three-story building, built for that purpose.
It was part of a big half of a block where the major-- the major shul was, which was beautiful.
I could show you some pictures maybe, because we were there a few years ago.
And there was also the offices of the community.
You know, the Jewish community had offices.
There were-- there were a lot of administration there, too, and the school.
And-- and the slaughter houses, as I told you, where they, you know, the kosher-- only-- only for poultry, not for-- not for cows, not for cattle.
So it was a-- a big thing and a very nice building, was very well-equipped.
And-- and teachers whom we looked up to, who were not mortals.
You had more than a religious education.
You had some secular education.
Yes, of course.
The-- it was a Jewish school, but part of it was secular education.
That's where I learned to-- to read and write and everything.
And then, you could continue in that school through seven grade, but I didn't.
I wanted to go onto some higher education.
So after fourth grade, I went to the [INAUDIBLE].
It was called [INAUDIBLE].
It's the name of a Romanian hero-- the wife of a Romanian hero.
The school still stands today, because I have seen it a few years ago, when I was in Satu Mare.

And it was something I was very proud of, that I could, because it was a rare thing that a daughter of such an Orthodox family as ours should go to that [INAUDIBLE], because I had to go to school and Sabbath on Saturday.
But we were exempt from writing.
It was accepted that Jewish girls did not write on Saturday.
But we had to carry our books, of course.
And I don't know if you are aware that Orthodox law forbids carrying on the Shabbat, only if you have that eruv.
--within that area, [? which is-- ?] So we were allowed to carry, since I remember in Satu Mare, because it did have the eruv.
But before the Germans came in, a couple of years ago, there was an anti-Semitic government empowered in Romania.
It was called the Black Guard.
And they removed the eruv.
So I was-- I wasn't allowed to carry my books.
So the maid, [INAUDIBLE], who wasn't Jewish, carried my books.
But when I came home, I didn't bring in my books in the house, because usually the family was sitting already at lunch, you know.
After shul, they came home and they were having lunch.
And for my father, that would have been something disturbing.
So I-- I left my books by a friend, a neighbor girl.
And I came in, and then I undressed because I wore a uniform.
In [INAUDIBLE], we had to wear a uniform.
And not only uniform, but we had a number on our arm.
Because we were allowed to-- we were-- we were not allowed to frequent certain places.
We-- we couldn't go to a movie which was not fit for a young girl.
We were not allowed to go to a theater.
We were not allowed to be out late in the night.
So if somebody spotted you, and you were in your uniform, marked down your number, and-- and presented it to the school there.
All the students, no.
This was-- this-- the Jewish girls were only a very small percentage of the school.
And so I took off my uniform, because this was not Shabbos day.
And I put on my civilian clothes.
And I went to sit down to have lunch.
That was-- that was-- yes, you could call it progressive.
That was something that he bowed to my mother's wishes.
And my parents were influenced by the director of the-- of the elementary school.
I was a good student.
And he said that that would be a shame, that I should not continue to higher education.
I had dreams of becoming a-- a lawyer, an attorney.
I hope so.
I-- I had a gift of gab.
I was, as you see, very short.
But they put me on a stool, and that's how I recited poems and that we had to memorize, you know, and even in kindergarten.
And I mastered the Romanian language very well.
Many didn't want to believe that I am a Jewess, that I am not a Romanian girl.
So whenever there was something to take care of in offices and things, I was the one who did it.
My parents spoke-- we spoke very little, which was rather than usual in a-- in such an Orthodox home.
But we spoke-- my father spoke very well Hungarian, so in the house, because he grew up under the Austro-Hungarian empire, rule.
And I spoke Romanian in school.
I went to school from 8 o'clock to 1 o'clock.
Afternoons were spent with homework.
It was a tremendous amount of homework.
So in the house, as I mentioned, we had maids.
And they came from my mother's village, from [INAUDIBLE], who spoke Romanian.
So I had the chance to-- to master it.
And as I told you, we girls spoke little Yiddish.
I speak better Yiddish today than I spoke at that time, because I use it more.
But my vocabulary is limited.
Let's talk a little bit about something you talked about, the Jewish Administration area building-- the Jewish Authority.
Talk a little bit about that, because that's unusual.
That's something that Americans aren't used to at all.
Well, we were-- yes, you are right.
The Orthodox scene in America is so very different.
But it's true, by the sheer fact-- Los Angeles and New York-- I mean, Satu Mare was-- the Jewish population of Satu Mare could-- could fit in two blocks maybe in-- in Brooklyn.
Or-- so everything was under one administration.
The rabbis were paid by-- by-- you paid the tax, every Jew paid the tax.
And the rabbis were-- were paid a salary from that administration.
So were the-- the school teachers, we-- we also paid the tuition for the elementary-- even the elementary school wasn't free.
The Jewish school you had to pay a tuition.
Well, they had to-- There was no other means for-- to keep it up, to support it.
And-- and although there were several smaller shuls, and they were-- how shall I tell you-- not the rabbis, but the [INAUDIBLE], who was like a judge.

When you had a-- a problem, questions about kashrut, about, you know, problems, questions about Jewish law, all kinds of situations, you went to that judge-like person, to the [INAUDIBLE], who you presented the problem.
And he gave you advice and a decision.
So everybody was paid from-- by-- by that the administration, by that community.
It wasn't like-- like in Los Angeles, you have 100 this small shuls, and each one has their own rabbi, and-- and each rabbi is supported by the members of that.
There-- there-- that wasn't like that.
When you say you paid a tax, did you pay the tax to the-- to the secular authorities, to the city authorities?
No, no.
You paid the-- this was Jewish.
And they took care of, for example, baking the matzohs before Pesach.
They had a big bakery, [? down ?] in a basement.
And it was also taken care of by the community.
It was interesting that you had to pay for the matzohs.
But for the matzohs, you paid according to your means.

Those who were more-- more-- how should I tell you-- who were more affluent, paid more, and those who made less paid less, and some who couldn't pay, in order they should be able to provide matzohs for everybody--
--for those who were not-- There-- there was a president of the community, the [INAUDIBLE], it's called in Jewish.
And then there secretaries, and then there were members of the committee who all decided that.
And it was, of course, a big honor to be the president or part of that committee.
Or-- so--
No, not at all.
It's-- it-- it took quite a while until we got used to-- to the system in America.
But again, you know, it's something that wouldn't be possible in America with the hundreds of thousands of Jewish population, like in Los Angeles or New York.
No, you go to the Jewish court.
You know about that.
It's called a [INAUDIBLE].
Yes, it's a Jewish court.
And there were-- pardon me.
And there were some judges, like, who were very good.
They had a lot of insight.
And you had to know all the intricate details of-- of-- of business and of laws.
So some were better than others.
But it was.
And usually, they obeyed by their decision.
Well, unfortunately no.

Because if there was something, which was rare, an embezzlement, burglary, a-- a-- a robbery or something, which was very rare, of course it was-- it was persecuted-- it was by the secular authorities, by the government.
But if you had a difference, business-wise, or-- or husband and wife problems, you did go to the Jewish, especially the Orthodox.
But Satu Mare had not only Orthodox Jews.
OK.
